title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Ethics of Facial Recognition Technology

texts.A: The deployment of facial recognition in public spaces is an unacceptable infringement on the right to anonymity. Unlike a physical ID, your face cannot be left at home. This technology turns every citizen into a permanent suspect, tracked by invisible algorithms [37]. While proponents argue it aids in catching criminals, the cost to our civil liberties is far too high. The history of surveillance states demonstrates that once data collection infrastructure is in place, the temptation to expand its scope proves irresistible to those in power. We are sleepwalking into a surveillance state where the 'chilling effect' on free assembly and protest will be irreversible. [38]A total ban on its use by law enforcement is the only way to protect the democratic fabric of our society.[/38] Incremental restrictions and oversight mechanisms are demonstrably insufficient, as decades of intelligence agency reform have repeatedly shown. Only a complete legislative prohibition can prevent the normalization of biometric mass surveillance.
texts.B: Facial recognition is a powerful tool for public safety that has been unfairly demonized. In an era of globalized threats, with terrorist networks and sophisticated criminal organizations operating across national boundaries, the ability to identify known dangerous individuals in a crowd is a vital necessity [40]. I disagree with the notion that it inherently destroys privacy; rather, it modernizes it. When used with strict judicial oversight and robust audit mechanisms, it is no different from any other forensic tool that law enforcement has incorporated over the past century. The focus should be on 'algorithmic auditing' to ensure accuracy across all demographics [39], not on banning a technology that can prevent tragedies before they occur. Accountability, transparency, and rigorous testing across diverse population datasets will ensure that this tool serves justice rather than undermines it. To refuse these tools on principle is to handicap the very institutions charged with keeping our communities safe, a trade-off that the public consistently rejects when presented with clear evidence of the technology's effectiveness.
texts.C: I share the view that the 'black box' nature of these algorithms is a major concern, as they often exhibit significant racial and gender bias [39]. These documented disparities disproportionately harm already marginalized communities and can lead to wrongful identification, with devastating consequences for innocent individuals. However, I disagree that a total ban is the solution; prohibition would simply push development into less regulated jurisdictions while depriving democratic societies of potential security benefits. We should implement a 'moratorium'—a temporary halt—until robust international standards for data protection and accuracy are established through genuine multilateral dialogue. I also share the concern about the 'chilling effect' on public life [37], but I believe that with clear 'no-go zones' for the technology and meaningful democratic oversight, we can strike a defensible balance between legitimate security imperatives and the freedom of the streets. Thoughtful co-existence with this technology—governed by enforceable standards, meaningful sanctions for misuse, and genuine democratic scrutiny—is both achievable and necessary in a world where the security threats are real.
texts.D: The real danger of facial recognition is not just the surveillance, but the 'predictive policing' it enables. When combined with big data analytics and geospatial tracking, it attempts to judge a person's intent before they have even acted, fundamentally inverting the presumption of innocence. I share the view that this technology is currently a threat to public safety due to its technical flaws [40]. However, we must recognize that the private sector's use of this tech—in our phones, banks, and retail environments—is already ubiquitous, rendering calls for a blanket ban both impractical and incoherent. The focus must be on 'data sovereignty,' giving individuals the power to opt-out and delete their biometric templates from any database [38]. Empowering citizens rather than banning technologies is the approach most likely to produce lasting, enforceable protection in a landscape where biometric data is already deeply embedded in our daily digital lives. Structural reform grounded in individual rights, not structural prohibition driven by worst-case scenarios, is the approach most likely to endure and to command lasting public confidence.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the 'chilling effect' of facial recognition on public behavior?
questions.explanation: Expert A mentions the 'chilling effect' on free assembly. Expert C explicitly states: 'I also share the concern about the chilling effect on public life.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on the best way to handle personal biometric data?
questions.explanation: Expert D suggests 'data sovereignty' and 'opt-out' options. Expert A, however, argues for a 'total ban,' implying that individual control is insufficient to prevent the surveillance state.

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to B regarding the need for technical accuracy and auditing?
questions.explanation: Expert B calls for 'algorithmic auditing' to ensure accuracy. Expert C also notes that algorithms 'often exhibit significant... bias' and calls for standards of accuracy.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the current reliability or necessity of the technology for law enforcement?
questions.explanation: Expert B calls it a 'vital necessity.' Expert D argues it is 'currently a threat to public safety due to its technical flaws.'
